case_name: Mevinphos removal from surface water for drinking water production with minimal chemical use
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for removing Mevinphos from surface water intended for drinking water under a minimal-chemical-use operating preference.

input_data.scenario_background: A small drinking water utility draws from a river reservoir affected by seasonal agricultural runoff. Monitoring detected Mevinphos at levels requiring targeted control before distribution. The utility prefers established treatment options that minimize routine chemical addition while maintaining reliable drinking water production.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve strong removal of Mevinphos and comply with drinking water quality expectations for safe potable use.
secondary_goals: maintain low to moderate operational complexity
use treatment approaches with established drinking water application

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: limited preference for processes that avoid large volumes of chemical sludge or hazardous regenerant waste
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: stable performance needed during seasonal runoff episodes

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove coarse debris and protect downstream units
core_unit_functions.clarification_or_media_filtration: reduce turbidity and particle loading to protect adsorptive treatment
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb dissolved Mevinphos and provide polishing for trace organic contaminants
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final pathogen inactivation for drinking water safety

applicability_rationale: Mevinphos is an organic pesticide for which activated carbon is a well-established control option in drinking water treatment
the stated preference for minimal chemical use favors physical adsorption over chemically intensive oxidation or coagulation-based specialty treatment
moderate source-water turbidity can be handled with simple solids removal ahead of GAC to preserve carbon bed life
this treatment train is compatible with conventional surface water treatment practice and provides a clear barrier for both micropollutants and pathogens
potential_risks: natural organic matter can compete for adsorption sites and shorten GAC run time
breakthrough of Mevinphos can occur if bed replacement or reactivation is delayed
if upstream particle removal is weak, carbon fouling and headloss may increase

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove coarse solids from raw surface water
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: lower suspended solids and improve downstream treatment stability
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon: adsorb dissolved Mevinphos during contact period
core_unit_functions.final_filtration: capture PAC-associated solids and polish treated water
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety in finished water

applicability_rationale: PAC is a recognized option for episodic pesticide contamination events in surface waters
it can be attractive where contamination is seasonal and the utility wants to avoid permanent high-pressure membrane systems
chemical use remains moderate relative to advanced oxidation or heavy coagulant dependence, especially for intermittent deployment
the process is feasible with standard operator skill if dosing and contact control are maintained
potential_risks: PAC performance depends strongly on dose, contact time, and competition from DOC
spent carbon solids increase residuals handling requirements
short-term contamination peaks may be missed if monitoring is infrequent

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: protect membrane equipment from large debris
core_unit_functions.low_pressure_membrane_filtration: provide robust particulate and microbial removal and stabilize influent to carbon
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbial safety barrier and distribution system protection

applicability_rationale: combining membrane pretreatment with GAC can produce very stable water quality for surface water applications
the dissolved pesticide is still primarily addressed by activated carbon, while the membrane improves particulate control
this option is technically strong but somewhat more energy- and maintenance-intensive than conventional filtration plus GAC
it remains compatible with the minimal-chemical-use preference because it limits reliance on coagulants
potential_risks: membranes do not directly remove dissolved Mevinphos effectively without the GAC step
membrane fouling control and cleaning increase O&M demands
higher capital cost may be unnecessary for a straightforward single-contaminant case

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove large debris from raw water
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize susceptible organic contaminants and improve downstream biodegradability
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: adsorb residual Mevinphos and biodegrade oxidized organic byproducts

applicability_rationale: ozone combined with BAC can address some pesticides and provide broader organic polishing
this route may be considered where utilities want added control of taste, odor, or multiple trace organics
however, it uses more energy and process sophistication than needed for the stated easy single-contaminant scenario
the minimal-chemical-use preference is only partly met because ozone generation and control add operational burden even if bulk chemical storage is limited
potential_risks: oxidation may not fully mineralize Mevinphos and can create transformation products requiring further polishing
ozone systems require careful safety management and skilled operation
bromate formation risk must be checked if bromide is present

core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide broad rejection of dissolved contaminants including many organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust corrosivity and mineral balance after desalting treatment
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety in finished water and distribution

applicability_rationale: RO is technically capable of removing many pesticides including trace dissolved organics
it is not the preferred first-line solution for a single pesticide problem in surface water when lower-energy adsorption options are available
the process conflicts with the utility preference for minimal operational burden and moderate costs
RO is generally better justified when salinity or multiple difficult dissolved contaminants must be treated simultaneously
potential_risks: concentrate management is difficult for inland utilities
surface water pretreatment demands are substantial to prevent membrane fouling
finished water remineralization and corrosion control add complexity

preferred_rank_pattern: activated_carbon_based treatment should rank first for Mevinphos in drinking water surface water applications
minimal_chemical_use preference should favor GAC or PAC over chemically intensive oxidation-first approaches
high-pressure membrane options should not rank first for this single-contaminant case

must_reflect: preference for minimal routine chemical addition
surface water still requires a clear microbial control barrier
proven adsorption-based treatment should be prioritized over unnecessarily complex advanced systems

must_contain: why activated carbon is appropriate for an organophosphate pesticide such as Mevinphos
why pretreatment for turbidity and natural organic matter matters for adsorption performance
why ozone-based or membrane-based systems rank lower under the stated constraints

should_mention: competition from DOC or NOM for carbon adsorption sites
possible oxidation byproduct concerns if advanced oxidation is proposed
concentrate or residuals management burdens for lower-ranked options
